skill.name: ai-avatar-video
skill.description: Guides users through creating AI avatar, talking head, lipsync, and dubbing videos with inference.sh CLI apps such as OmniHuman, Fabric, PixVerse, Kokoro TTS, Whisper, and LatentSync.
skill.summary: Create AI avatar and lipsync video workflows with inference.sh CLI models.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer correctly found external command and network patterns in SKILL.md. Most command hits are fenced CLI examples for the declared inference.sh workflow, but the installer uses a curl-to-shell command at line 21, which is a high-risk supply-chain pattern that should be changed before publication.

high_findings.title: Pipe to Shell Installer Pattern
high_findings.description: SKILL.md recommends running curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh && infsh login. Piping remote network content directly into a shell can execute changed or compromised installer code without user review, creating a supply-chain execution risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present in the quick start. This is a well-known risky installation pattern even when the referenced service is legitimate.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Execute Remote AI Workflows
medium_findings.description: The skill relies on infsh app run and npx skills add examples across SKILL.md. This matches the skill purpose, but it causes command execution and remote service interaction, so users should inspect commands and understand what files or media URLs they submit.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible examples for a CLI-based skill and are not hidden malware. Risk remains because following them invokes external tools and cloud services.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example URLs and Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs mostly point to inference.sh documentation or placeholder media such as https://portrait.jpg and https://speech.mp3. These are expected for examples, but users may upload sensitive media to external services if they replace placeholders with private URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly observable and are mainly placeholders or official documentation. The residual risk is data exposure through user-provided media links.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md line 3, but that line begins the YAML description block. No evidence found of cryptographic code or algorithm selection in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is metadata text, not executable cryptographic logic. The finding appears to be caused by keyword matching rather than semantic evidence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Shell
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start pipes an installer downloaded over HTTPS directly into sh. Replace this with a verifiable install process that lets users inspect the script, verify checksums, or use a package manager.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command combines network retrieval and shell execution on one line. This pattern is explicitly present and materially increases execution risk.

content.value_statement: Teams need fast ways to turn portraits, scripts, audio, and existing videos into presenter-style content. This skill provides CLI workflows for avatar videos, lipsync, dubbing, and related media steps.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh avatar and lipsync apps for model discovery.
Shows OmniHuman 1.5 and OmniHuman 1.0 avatar video commands.
Provides Fabric 1.0 and PixVerse Lipsync command examples.
Combines text-to-speech output with avatar video generation.
Outlines a dubbing workflow using transcription, translation, speech, and lipsync.
Links to inference.sh documentation for running apps and streaming results.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account access, and working model availability.
Uses external cloud services for media processing and model execution.
Does not provide consent, likeness rights, or content policy review.
Example media URLs are placeholders that users must replace with valid assets.

use_cases.title: Product Demo Presenter
use_cases.description: Create a consistent AI presenter for product walkthroughs, launches, and short promotional videos.

use_cases.title: Course Explainer Video
use_cases.description: Turn lesson scripts and presenter images into talking head videos for online courses.

use_cases.title: Localized Video Dubbing
use_cases.description: Plan a workflow that transcribes source media, creates translated speech, and syncs it to video.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Avatar Clip
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one portrait and one audio file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create an avatar video from my portrait URL and audio URL. Recommend the simplest model and show the CLI command.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Lipsync Models
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose between available avatar and lipsync options.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare OmniHuman 1.5, Fabric 1.0, and PixVerse Lipsync for a realistic talking head video. Recommend one for my use case.

prompt_templates.title: Build a TTS Avatar Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate speech first, then use it for an avatar video.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a text-to-speech and avatar workflow for this script. Include the inference.sh commands and the expected handoff between steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Dubbing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a dubbing workflow for an existing video in another language. Include transcription, translation, new speech, lipsync, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: I have a presenter photo and a narration audio file for a product demo.
output_examples.output: Recommended path: use OmniHuman 1.5 with the portrait and audio URLs. Prepare a front-facing image, clean audio, and review the generated clip for likeness accuracy.

output_examples.input: I need to localize an existing training video into Spanish.
output_examples.output: Suggested workflow: transcribe the original audio, translate the script, create Spanish narration, then run a lipsync model against the original video.

output_examples.input: I want the most realistic lipsync result for a short social video.
output_examples.output: Start with PixVerse Lipsync or OmniHuman 1.5. Use high-resolution source media and clear audio to reduce mouth-shape errors.

best_practices: Use only media that you own or have permission to process.
Inspect install and CLI commands before running them locally.
Use clear audio and front-facing images for better lipsync results.
anti_patterns: Do not create avatar videos from a person without consent.
Do not upload private media URLs unless the service terms allow it.
Do not pipe remote installers to a shell without verification.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps create AI avatar videos, talking head clips, lipsync videos, and dubbing workflows using inference.sh CLI apps.

faq.question: Does it generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. The examples use the inference.sh CLI to call external model services.

faq.question: Which models are covered?
faq.answer: The skill references OmniHuman 1.5, OmniHuman 1.0, Fabric 1.0, PixVerse Lipsync, Kokoro TTS, Whisper, and LatentSync.

faq.question: Can it create audio for avatars?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow that uses text-to-speech before creating the avatar video.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with private media?
faq.answer: Use caution. The workflow sends media URLs to external services, so review privacy rules and service terms first.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk rating high?
faq.answer: The quick start includes a remote installer piped directly to a shell, which is risky until replaced with a verifiable install method.
